utterances: Hubbard, are you there?
Yes, sir.
You're on the air.
Please go ahead.
I'd like to make two comments, if I would, if I could.
One of them is what a fine veterans hospital we have in Lake City, Florida.
I had to have an eye removed over there, and I couldn't have asked for any better service anywhere.
And I know quite a few veterans that go there, and they all get the same service.
People there just bend over backwards to help you and make sure you get what you need.
And I just wanted next to say that Walter Reed is not the - the other hospitals are not all just like Walter Reed.
Well, let me ask the respond to that by reading this e-mail.
This -excuse me, I think this actually posted at our blog, at the Blog of the Nation.
Please ask the journalists to comment on the distinction between military hospitals, such as Walter Reed, and the veterans administration system.

It's my understanding that the hospitals, the problems they uncovered are prevalent in the military hospital system but that the VA, which is of course what Hubbard is talking about, a distinct system, is a lot better.
Is that accurate, do you think, Anne Hull?
Well, we've not done - we focused on the Walter Reed situation.
And after that story published, tons of tons, I'm talking thousands of e-mails and calls came in, and people wanted to say how their military facilities were.
And we heard lots of bad stories, and then of course that broadened out to people reporting stories about their own VA hospitals, which is a separate system from the military hospitals.
But what we're concerned about is the big hand-off that's going on now from the Army into the Veterans Affairs hospitals.
They are about to get the mighty deluge of all these casualties from Iraq and Afghanistan and already seeing the early waves of that.
Some soldiers we followed from Walter Reed are now in the VA system and are having to wait six months to get their initial appointment.
Hubbard, though, you wanted to say your experience was very good.
Yes, and I'd like to make another comment, if I might.
Please go ahead.
If that man in charge of Walter Reed had been an enlisted man, he would have been charged with dereliction of duty, sentenced and probably do some jail time.
That general is going to get put in an office job somewhere, either that or retire early and won't lose one dime.
And he won't even get a reprimand probably.
So that's one thing that's wrong with what's going on in a hospital like Walter Reed.
Well, Staff Sergeant Dan Shannon said it best yesterday.
He said if I were in the field and screwing up, I wouldn't be able to resign.
I would have to finish my duties.
Hubbard, thanks very much for the call.
And we're glad things went well with your eye surgery.
Thank you, sir.
Appreciate it.
Let's see if we can get to - this is Jeff(ph), Jeff calling us from here in Washington, D.C.

I was a soldier assigned to Walter Reed during the beginning part of the war, and I can honestly say that the care that I was able to provide was unparalleled to any type of care that I could provide at any civilian hospital.
I am a registered nurse, and I was absolutely proud to serve each one of the soldiers that I came in contact with.
And so you're suggesting that, at least from your experience, the care they got at these facilities was first rate.

In my opinion, in the intensive care unit, because I wasn't really assigned work to have to deal with the outpatient-type facilities - the care that I was able to provide, in my opinion, was top-notch.
I mean I've worked at some great facilities in the Northeastern part of the United States, and the support I got from my military head nurse was stellar.
The physicians, actually the nurses and physicians, we would come in on our days off.
I mean, we really did the best we could for the soldier.
The issue that I have is that the facility itself is old and dilapidated, and I believe that's why they decided to BRAC it.
BRAC means put it on the Base Realignment and Closing commission and close it, yes.
Yes.
Well…
Sorry.
Go ahead, Dana.
Well we heard the same thing.

We heard that medical care was top-notch, that doctors and nurses and the support staff within the hospital, even though they had to deal with intermittent water shortages or no hot water and, like you said, an old building, that the care given there was excellent.
We really were not dealing with that.
We were dealing with the environment that exists once you leave the hospital as an in-patient.
You become an out-patient, you know, go home.

You still live within the confines of Walter Reed or nearby, and that there are 700 of these outpatients who come back sometimes to the hospital but more likely to the rehab clinics to get care or counseling of some sort.
So we really tried in the stories to make a distinction that we were not talking about the medical care at the hospital, which, again, most people were very happy with.

Yes, ma'am, and my problem is I think that the American public is hearing tragedy at Walter Reed or soldiers cared for poorly at Walter Reed, and they're assuming that the medical, nursing and care that all the enlisted nurses and technicians provide is sub-standard, and it really is starting to I think not necessarily affect everyone the same way.
I mean, I am obviously very hurt that this is happening to our young men and women.

I mean, in my opinion, these soldiers who go to Iraq, go to Afghanistan, deserve everything that they have coming to them insofar as they need a higher level of care, they should have, in my opinion, full retirements and benefits.
But I just think that America may be getting the wrong message when you're talking about the care versus the actual facilities that the soldiers are staying in, and with that I will let you go.
I appreciate your letting me make my comments.
Jeff, thanks very much.
We're talking with Washington Post reporters Dana Priest and Anne Hull about the scandal they uncovered at Walter Reed Army Medical Center.
You're listening to TALK OF THE NATION from NPR News.
And let me ask you, Dana Priest, where this story goes from here in the sense - we've obviously seen Congress get involved.
Is it becoming a politicized story?
As you said, the president came out today with some very strong remarks and said that he's appointing this special commission to investigate what happened.
Yesterday, Vice President Cheney also made some very strong remarks.
This is going to be fixed.
No excuses will be accepted.
The red tape will be cut through.
Well, I think it already has become a political situation, a political story, and that the White House is struggling mightily to contain it.
The Democrats have, just last week - it took them a couple days to sort of rally, but then they did and they launched, you know, two investigations on the Hill and one at Walter Reed.
They want their own independent commission.
Many different members of the Democratic Congress issued statements, and of course they're pointing to the administration, Republican administration, to criticize.
All right.
So the White House knows that, and so to the Republicans on the Hill, and they're fighting back, I would say, by trying to get ahead of it in one way.

The fact that Bush came out and made some remarks in his radio address, Cheney came out and said what he said, which is basically, you know, that it needed to be fixed and it was somehow just the federal bureaucracy's problem, and Bush today reassuring veterans, who were among their greatest supporters of the war, some of them, that they will get this done.
And I think now he's taken the steps that show that he is willing to get this done by appointing this new commission and by speaking publicly about it.

So I think their effort is to contain it, to take away from the Democrats any kind of ammunition that they might have to continue to say that the administration is not doing right by veterans or the wounded - by doing everything they can think of for the wounded and holding people accountable so that they can't say, like they did at Abu Ghraib, you know, you're only taking down lower-ranking people.
If you remember, that did happen at Abu Ghraib.
There were no general officers, or no actual even senior officers who were relieved over that and the Democrats made a lot of criticisms out of that fact.
Anne Hull, it's not just of course the White House that has to be careful about the politics of this.
Congress, too.
They did not - their role of oversight, well, it left a lot to be desired in this case.
But for any kind of teaching jobs and the economy in Michigan right now there are no - very few new teacher hires.
For example, in Troy there was three new elementary positions opened up, and there was 10,000 applicants.
Well, does the fact that the Iranians don't own up to what the IAEA has found - does that pose problems for the Iran deal, for the nuclear deal?

Well, actually the Oversight Committee did some good work for the last couple of years looking into issues of pay mix-ups - soldiers, Guard members were not getting their pay - medical record foul-ups, the communication problems between different branches of the service.
So some work has been done on this.
This is not a surprise to many members of Congress.

And as we found out this week in the thousands of e-mails we got, so many people said I have written my congressperson for years on veterans' issues especially, and a lot of wounded soldiers say they had contacted their member from their home district, too.
And Dana, I wanted to ask you.

Earlier, Anne mentioned the story that you guys wrote yesterday, where there was a lot of information about similar kinds of problems at other military hospitals and indeed at some veterans hospitals - again, two distinct systems.
Where does your investigation go from here?
We're not sure.
I think right now the effort is to just make sure that we've done everything we could here at Walter Reed and we know what we need to know.
And certainly we're watching how it develops, but it's unclear where it will go from here right now.
Dana Priest, thanks very much.
And of course, Anne Hull.
Thank you both for your time today.
We appreciate it.
Thank you.
Thank you.
The two reporters for the Washington Post who broke the scandal at the Walter Reed Army Medical Center joining us today from the offices of the Washington Post here in Washington, D.C.
